heading: DELL'ALTRO IN QUESTA VITA
card_summary: This very long chunk (opening portions summarised here) from the third Cantico's Sixth Tone develops Zorzi's systematic anthropology as imago Dei. It moves through five closely connected arguments: the inadequacy of the standard Augustinian memoria-intellectus-voluntas triad as an image of the Trinity; the catalogue of divine anthropomorphic members drawn from Psalms, Isaiah, Jeremiah, the Song of Songs, Habakkuk, and the Gospels; the Kabbalistic doctrine of God's ten "garments" (levushim), mapped onto the procession of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit and then onto seven further vestments governing God's relation to creatures (mercy/linen, justice/blood, tif'eret/beauty, two intimate garments likened to breasts, malkut/kingdom, and shekinah); man as microcosm who contains in act what God contains in power (citing Dionysius the Areopagite and Gregory the Great); and the soul placed in the body as in a crucible (vas fusorium) to be purified by the fire of charity, with the Pentecostal descent of the Spirit as the supreme instance of that cosmic sacrifice. Sources range from Aristotle and Plotinus on the soul's passions, Jerome's commentary on Habakkuk, Pseudo-Dionysius, Kabbalistic theology (the concept of the Ein Sof "naked God" and the sefirah Shekinah), and multiple Pauline letters.
essay:
# Dell'Altro in Questa Vita — Chunk 97 (pp. 2303–2374)

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (approximately the first 6,000 words / first five sub-sections of what appears to be a much longer text). The section continues beyond what is summarised here.*

## 1. The Imago Dei: Beyond the Augustinian Triad (Cap. I)

Zorzi opens by announcing his intention to explain how man corresponds to and is rendered similar to God. He immediately takes issue with the traditional Augustinian formula identifying the divine image with the three soul-faculties of memory (*memoria*), intellect (*intellectus*), and will (*voluntas*). This doctrine, he dismisses as a teaching "diffusa tra il volgo e persino tra le vecchierelle" ("widespread among the common people and even among old women"), which represents not a true image but at best a "vestige" (*orma*), since a vestige represents only a part of its original, not the whole.

More recent scholastic theologians, Zorzi notes, extend the triad to include "second acts" — the Word produced from the intellect and the Love proceeding from the will, corresponding to the Son and Holy Spirit respectively. But even this remains deficient: in us these are accidents, while in God they are substance identical with God himself. Memory in us is recollection of the past; in God it is the simultaneous vision of all things past, present, and future.

Zorzi's solution is to compare not merely soul but the whole man (*totus homo*) to God. The key analogy is the image of God as a circle (*circulus*) that takes its origin from itself and returns to itself, drawing all things with it — and man as a corresponding circle proceeding from God and returning to him. Man imitates God even in duration: unable to possess his eternity all at once (*tota simul*), he achieves it through perpetual succession.

> *Est insuper Deus circulus quidam, non aliunde quam a seipso trahens originem, et in seipsum tendens omnia illuc secum deducit. Est etiam homo circulus a Deo procedens, et in ipsum tendens, illuc omnia deducens cum omnia contineat.*

<cite>p. 2136 (Latin column)</cite>

Aristotle and Plotinus are cited on the soul's apparent emotions: to say the soul is sad or joyful is like saying it weaves or spins; it is the *animal* part that experiences passions, while soul and intellect direct the animal just as God directs the world through secondary causes.

## 2. God's Anthropomorphic Members (Cap. II)

Zorzi moves to the bold claim that God himself, by attributing bodily members to himself in Scripture, has authorised the search for his image in the human form "at least by a certain analogy" (*per analogiam quandam*). The prohibition on making images in the Law had two purposes: to prevent idolatry and to prevent a false representation of what cannot be depicted. Yet God's own testimony in Scripture overrides this prohibition.

Zorzi assembles an exhaustive Scriptural dossier of divine body-parts: right hand (Psalm 110), left hand (Matthew 25), sitting posture (Isaiah 66), walking at the breeze of the day (Genesis 3), steps and journeys (Psalm 68, Habakkuk 3 — glossed via Jerome as the destruction of demonic powers), eyes and ears (Psalm 34), eyelids (Psalm 11), nostrils, mouth (Isaiah), palate and throat (Song of Songs 7), head as *karmel* — which Zorzi argues means not the mountain but the purple-producing mollusc (an original philological point), shoulders (Isaiah 9; Luke 15 the lost sheep), arms (Isaiah 53, Exodus), heart (1 Kings 13), breast (John 13 — the Beloved Disciple), back (Jeremiah 2), feet (Psalm 18), wings (Psalm 18), and royal garments (Revelation 19).

The members are then interpreted symbolically: eyes = God's all-penetrating gaze; nostrils = discernment and reception of good works; ears = knowledge of the heart's most hidden thoughts; eyelids = the secret subtle judgments of God (citing Jerome); teeth = the grinding and distributing of multiple kinds of spiritual nourishment; shoulders = the property of the Son who bears all things (*Portans omnia verbo virtutis suae*, Hebrews 1:3); arm = miraculous power; heart = tender love; back = either indignation or the "posterior" that God showed Moses; feet = the "passions" by which he approaches or withdraws from us; wings = swiftness in responding to human need.

> *Membra itaque sunt vires multiplices in illo uno simplicissimo, et vestimenta viae quaedam, per quas graditur nobiscum, aut in nobis sese diffundit.*

<cite>p. 2142 (Latin column)</cite>

The Kabbalistic dimension enters here explicitly: "gli ebrei trattano con grande ampiezza di quegli indumenti nella loro teologia" — the "garments" (*vestimenta* / Hebrew *levushim*) are the forms through which divine power flows, since from the "naked God" (*Deus nudus*) — totally withdrawn from all relation — nothing flows, though he is the source of all goodness. Zorzi cites the Gospel episode of the haemorrhage-woman (Luke 8:46) as proof that power flows through the garments.

## 3. The Ten Divine Garments and Their Kabbalistic Mapping (Cap. III)

Cap. III is the theological centrepiece of the opening section. Zorzi states that the Hebrew theologians (i.e., Kabbalists) count up to ten garments (*usque ad decem vestimenta non sine sacramento*) — "not without a mystical meaning" — and that he would not credit them had Scripture not taught the same.

The "naked God" (*Deus nudus*) is defined as God considered in his solitary nature, utterly removed from all intrinsic and extrinsic relation. In this state God speaks in Isaiah: the Hebrew phrase *ani Adonay hu shemi* ("I am the Lord, this is my name") is parsed by Zorzi: the pronoun *hu* does not function relatively but indicates the absolute *ipsum*, "that which is in the supreme degree this." In this absolute selfhood God "does not communicate himself to anyone," so the verse continues: "my glory I will not give to another."

From this absolute unity God then generates the Son — "the first garment" — paternitas, which is an inaccessible light (citing James 1:17, "Father of lights"; Psalm 104:2, "clothed in light as in a garment"). The second garment is the reception of the Son in the paternal bosom, the supracelestial waters by which God is "clothed in waters" (Psalm 104:3), corresponding to the Son as Wisdom who is given "the water of saving wisdom" (Sirach 15:3). The third garment is the Holy Spirit, appearing in both Testaments as fire: the smoke of Sinai (Exodus 19), the fire on the mountain (Exodus 24), and Pentecost (Acts 2).

After this "most sacred, interior and perfect procession" (*sacratissima et intrinseca productio*), God assumes seven further garments to act with creatures:

1. **Mercy/goodness/piety** (*misericordia, clementia, bonitas, pietas*) — identified with the white byssus of Revelation 19:8 ("the just deeds of the saints")
2. **Justice** — appearing as either a blood-stained garment (Revelation 19:13) or linen; Zorzi cites Pseudo-Dionysius on the angels' question "why is your garment red?" and the figure of Gabriel in linen from Daniel
3. **Tif'eret** — Hebrew *tif'eret* (rendered in the Latin column with the actual Hebrew characters), glossed as "gloria et pulchritudo divina," corresponding to the garment of multicoloured embroidery given to Jerusalem in Ezekiel 16 and the Canticle's praise of the human creature as "most beautiful among women"
4–5. Two intimate garments of "celebration and splendour" (*confessio et decor*, Psalm 104:1), also called **breasts** (*ubera*) that nursed the prophets, identified with the third heaven to which Paul was rapt (2 Corinthians 12)
6. God as **Life, Tree of Life, Land of the Living** — garment of the abyss (Psalm 104:6)
7. **Malkut** (*malcut* / Hebrew *מלכות*) = "kingdom," the most intimate garment, also called **Shekinah** (*sechina* / Hebrew *שכינה*) = "the immediate indwelling of God in us," citing Jeremiah ("You are in our midst, O Lord"). The Shekinah as cloud protects humanity from the blaze of divine wrath and covers human shame; Ezekiel 16 ("I spread my cloak over you and covered your shame") is read as the supreme instance.

> *Denique summa haec divina maiestas ultimum sumit amictum, quo nobis penitus familiarem se praebeat. Et hoc Hebraei מלכות malcut id est regnum vocant... Nuncupatur quoque שכינה sechina id est inhabitatio Dei in nobis immediata.*

<cite>p. 2152–2153 (Latin column)</cite>

The section closes with the claim that the human body is the garment through which *all* of God's garments are represented: body-members are simultaneously called *membra*, *vestimenta*, and *mensurae* (dimensions/measures by which God measures and governs all things). Man, on this reading, carries an image of God more expressive than that of the angels, because his organic body with its articulations mirrors the full system of divine self-communication.

## 4. Man as Microcosm Reconciling All Things with God (Cap. IV)

Drawing on Pseudo-Dionysius and Gregory the Great, Zorzi argues that man contains in act what God contains in power and "by a certain eminence." When man is reconciled to God, all creation is reconciled — just as in the Incarnation the assumption of one human nature entailed the assumption of all things: soul, spirit, animal faculties, vegetative power, the elements, prime matter. The soteriological extension is universal: what happened in Christ can happen in every soul that receives the indwelling Christ (John 14:23), and the goal is the unity expressed in Christ's high-priestly prayer (John 17:21).

## 5. The Soul in the Body as Crucible (Cap. V)

The section concludes its opening movement with an eschatological and sacramental argument. The soul is placed in the body "as in a smelting-vessel" (*vas fusorium*) to be purified and fused by the fire of charity, divine grace adapting itself to the recipient's disposition, mediated above all by sacrifices and sacraments. The Latin term *holah* (Hebrew *עֹלָה*, "burnt offering, ascending") is cited as the technical name for the holocaust, which Zorzi allegorises as the complete transformation of the sacrificial matter into fire that ascends to God.

The Pentecost is read as the supreme historical enactment of this cosmic furnace: the fiery Spirit could not be sent until the Son had been offered as holocaust (John 16:7, "If I do not go, the Paraclete will not come"), and the fifty-day interval is held to be numerically appropriate to the fire-Spirit. The Pauline injunction to offer oneself as a "living, unspotted, holy sacrifice pleasing to God" (Romans 12:1) is the individual correlate of this cosmic process. The mortal and corruptible body, sown as an animal body, will rise as a spiritual body — the true holocaust, "burned by that divine fire that transforms all things."

> *Anima itaque in hoc corpore collocata est tamquam in vase fusorio collanda, et expurganda igne charitatis, et divino perfuso in toto homine per gratiam infundentis.*

<cite>p. 2156 (Latin column)</cite>

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section is a pivotal node in the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s anthropological programme. The ten divine garments serve as Zorzi's most extended and explicit alignment of Christian Trinitarian theology with Kabbalistic Sefirotic structure: the first three garments map the immanent Trinity (Father-Son-Spirit) while the seven extrinsic garments correspond to the seven sefirot of divine self-communication, climaxing in Malkut/Shekinah as the point of God's immediate presence in creation. The section also confirms Zorzi's characteristic method: Scripture (especially Psalms, Isaiah, the Song of Songs) is read through the combined lens of Kabbalistic letter-commentary, Neoplatonic emanation (the circle metaphor from Plotinus/Proclus), Pseudo-Dionysian hierarchy, and Scholastic theology of act and potency. The theme of man as the garment through which all divine garments are expressed ties anthropology directly to the cosmological harmonics of the earlier cantici.
